codeSnapshot.label: agent_loop.py
codeSnapshot.code:
from typing import Annotated
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.tools import tool
from langchain_core.messages import HumanMessage
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
from langgraph.graph.message import add_messages
from langgraph.prebuilt import ToolNode
from typing_extensions import TypedDict

# --- 1. Define a tool ---
@tool
def get_word_length(word: str) -> int:
    """Returns the number of characters in a word."""
    return len(word)

tools = [get_word_length]

# --- 2. Set up the LLM with tools bound ---
llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o", temperature=0)
llm_with_tools = llm.bind_tools(tools)

# --- 3. Define graph state ---
class State(TypedDict):
    messages: Annotated[list, add_messages]

# --- 4. Define the agent node (calls the LLM) ---
def agent_node(state: State) -> State:
    response = llm_with_tools.invoke(state["messages"])
    return {"messages": [response]}

# --- 5. Routing: continue looping if tool calls exist, else END ---
def should_continue(state: State) -> str:
    last_message = state["messages"][-1]
    if hasattr(last_message, "tool_calls") and last_message.tool_calls:
        return "tools"
    return END

# --- 6. Build the graph ---
tool_node = ToolNode(tools)

graph_builder = StateGraph(State)
graph_builder.add_node("agent", agent_node)
graph_builder.add_node("tools", tool_node)
graph_builder.set_entry_point("agent")
graph_builder.add_conditional_edges("agent", should_continue)
graph_builder.add_edge("tools", "agent")  # loop back after tool execution

graph = graph_builder.compile()

# --- 7. Run the agent loop ---
initial_state = {"messages": [HumanMessage(content="How many characters are in the word 'supercalifragilistic'?")]}

for step in graph.stream(initial_state, stream_mode="values"):
    last = step["messages"][-1]
    last.pretty_print()

eli3: An AI agent is a program that can take actions in the world, not just answer questions. The agent loop is the repeating cycle it follows: look at the situation, decide what to do, do it, then look again. It keeps going until the task is done or it gives up.

Think of it like a chef following a recipe: read the next step (observe), figure out which tool to grab (think), grab the pan and turn the heat on (act), then read the next step again. The loop repeats until the dish is finished.

In software terms: the LLM reads the current state (conversation history + tool outputs), generates a thought and a tool call, the framework executes the tool, and the result is added back to the context. This cycle repeats until the LLM emits a final answer with no tool call.

whenWhy.title: Tasks that require multiple sequential steps
whenWhy.body: Single LLM calls answer one question at a time. The agent loop enables tasks where step N depends on the output of step N-1: e.g., search for a paper → read its references → fetch and summarise each reference → compile a report. Each iteration of the loop brings the agent closer to the goal. Without the loop, multi-step tasks require manual orchestration by the caller.

whenWhy.title: Tasks that require tool use and external state
whenWhy.body: Agents interact with external systems (web search, code execution, databases, APIs). The loop manages the cycle of: LLM decides which tool to call → framework executes the tool → result is injected back into the LLM's context. This is the core pattern behind coding agents, research agents, and customer-support agents. The loop continues until the LLM decides it has enough information to respond.

whenWhy.title: Tasks requiring error recovery and replanning
whenWhy.body: In a multi-step agentic task, individual steps fail: API calls timeout, code throws exceptions, search returns irrelevant results. The loop enables recovery: the tool error is added to the context, the LLM observes the failure, and it can replan (retry with different parameters, try a different tool, or ask for clarification). A single-shot LLM call cannot recover from tool failures; the loop is what makes agents resilient.

deepDiveIntro: The agent loop is the fundamental execution model for autonomous AI agents: a repeating cycle in which the agent observes its current state, reasons about what to do next, and takes an action — then observes the result and repeats. This Observe-Think-Act cycle is what distinguishes a reactive agent from a single-shot LLM call. Every major agent framework (LangChain, LlamaIndex, Autogen, Anthropic's agent SDK) is built around this loop, even when the surface API varies significantly.

The canonical formalisation comes from ReAct (Yao et al. 2022): at each step t, the agent produces a Thought (natural-language reasoning trace) and an Action (tool call or final answer). The environment executes the action and returns an Observation. The sequence (Thought, Action, Observation) is appended to the prompt and the LLM is called again. Modern frameworks implement this with a maximum iteration limit and early-stopping when the LLM emits a terminal action. In asynchronous multi-agent systems, multiple sub-loops can run concurrently, each sharing results through a shared memory or message-passing layer. [1]

deepDive.heading: Loop Structure: Observe-Think-Act
deepDive.body: At each iteration: (1) Observe — assemble the current context: system prompt, conversation history, all previous (Thought, Action, Observation) triples; (2) Think — call the LLM with the assembled context; the LLM outputs a reasoning trace and either a tool call or a final answer; (3) Act — if a tool call was emitted, execute it (web search, code interpreter, database query, etc.) and record the observation; append (Thought, Action, Observation) to history; goto (1). If a final answer was emitted, return it to the caller. The loop terminates on: final answer, max_iterations exceeded, or hard timeout. [1]

deepDive.heading: Context Window Management in Long Loops
deepDive.body: Each iteration appends ~500–2000 tokens to the context. Over many steps, the context window fills and older steps are truncated or summarised. Strategies: (1) Sliding window — keep the last N observations and drop older ones; (2) Summary memory — after K steps, summarise the history with another LLM call and replace it with the summary; (3) External memory — offload observations to a vector store and retrieve relevant ones at each step rather than keeping the full history in-context. Context overflow is one of the primary failure modes for long-horizon agents. [2]

deepDive.heading: Loop Termination and Safety
deepDive.body: Without termination guards, agents can loop indefinitely (hallucinated tool calls, circular reasoning, tool always returning errors). Production safeguards: max_iterations (hard cap, typically 10–25 for single agents), wall-clock timeout, budget limits (max LLM API cost per task), semantic stopping (detect repeated identical actions — a sign of a stuck loop), and human-in-the-loop checkpoints for high-stakes actions (file deletion, sending emails, financial transactions). [2]

deepDive.heading: Termination Conditions and Loop Safety
deepDive.body: A well-designed agent loop must define explicit termination conditions beyond the happy path of a final answer. Production systems enforce multiple independent safety layers: a hard max_iterations cap (typically 10–25) as the last resort, a wall-clock or per-task budget timeout, and semantic cycle detection that inspects the last N (Thought, Action) pairs for repetition — a reliable signal that the agent is stuck. Per-tool error budgets add another layer: if the same tool returns consecutive errors, the loop surfaces a structured failure to the caller rather than burning iterations on futile retries. Human-in-the-loop checkpoints are inserted before irreversible actions (file writes, financial transactions, email sends), requiring explicit approval before the loop continues. These guards belong in the framework layer — never rely solely on the LLM to decide when to stop, as this is a known failure mode under adversarial inputs or prompt injection attacks. [2]

deepDive.heading: Termination Conditions and Safety Limits
deepDive.body: Beyond a simple max_iterations cap, production agent loops require a layered termination strategy. First, a hard iteration ceiling (10–25 steps is typical) acts as the unconditional last resort — when hit, the loop aborts and surfaces a structured error with the partial state, so callers can inspect what happened. Second, infinite-loop detection compares each (action, arguments) pair against recent history using a rolling hash; if the same call has been made K consecutive times (K=2–3), the loop is declared stuck and terminated early, saving both compute and cost. Third, token budget tracking accumulates prompt + completion tokens across all iterations and halts when a per-task ceiling is reached — essential for cost control in high-volume production systems. Fourth, error recovery distinguishes transient failures (network timeout, rate limit) — handled with exponential backoff up to a per-tool retry budget — from terminal failures (resource not found, authentication failure) that should short-circuit the loop immediately. The correct pattern is always to return errors as tool result messages so the LLM can observe and replan, rather than raising uncaught exceptions that crash the loop and lose state. Finally, for high-stakes or irreversible actions (database writes, financial transactions, external emails), insert explicit human-in-the-loop checkpoints: the loop pauses, presents the proposed action to a human approver, and only continues on explicit approval. Never rely on the LLM's own judgment as the sole termination guard — this is a known failure mode under adversarial prompts. [2]

deepDive.heading: Stateful vs Stateless Agent Loops
deepDive.body: A stateless agent loop re-assembles the full conversation history from scratch at each invocation: the caller stores and passes in every prior (Thought, Action, Observation) triple. This is simple to implement but puts the state management burden on the caller and requires re-transmitting the full history on every step, which becomes expensive as context grows. LangGraph implements a stateful loop via a typed State object that persists across nodes: each node reads from and writes to a shared state dict, and the framework manages routing based on state values rather than explicit return values. State flows through nodes as a pure data structure (TypedDict in Python), making it easy to inspect, log, and replay. Checkpointing goes further: LangGraph supports persisting the full graph state to an external store (SQLite, Redis, Postgres) after each node execution. This enables fault tolerance (the loop can resume from the last checkpoint if the process crashes), human-in-the-loop interrupts (the loop halts at a designated checkpoint and waits for human input before continuing), and multi-session persistence (the same agent can pause and resume across multiple user sessions). The checkpointer is configured at graph compilation time via the `checkpointer` argument and is transparent to node implementations — nodes read and write state without knowing it is being persisted. For stateful, long-running, or multi-session agents, checkpointing is essential infrastructure; for short, single-session tasks, a stateless in-memory loop is simpler and sufficient. [2]

takeaways: Agent loop: Observe (assemble context) → Think (LLM call) → Act (execute tool) → repeat until final answer or termination. [1]
ReAct formalises the loop as interleaved Thought/Action/Observation triples in the LLM prompt. [1]
Context window grows with each iteration; long tasks require sliding window, summarisation, or external memory. [2]
Termination guards (max_iterations, timeout, budget, cycle detection) are essential for production agents. [2]
The loop is the minimal substrate for multi-step, tool-using, error-recovering AI agents. [1]

edgeCases.q: What happens when the agent gets stuck in an infinite tool-call loop?
edgeCases.a: Stuck loops occur when: (1) the tool always returns an error and the LLM keeps retrying; (2) the LLM hallucinates a tool call that doesn't exist; (3) the LLM produces the same action repeatedly (semantic loop). Defences: (a) max_iterations hard cap — always set this; (b) action deduplication — if the last N actions are identical, force-terminate with an error; (c) error budget — if the same tool returns errors K times consecutively, abort and surface the error to the caller; (d) per-tool retry limits. Never rely solely on the LLM to self-terminate — always enforce hard limits in the framework layer.

edgeCases.q: How do you make the agent loop work with streaming LLM responses?
edgeCases.a: With streaming, the LLM emits tokens incrementally. The framework must buffer the stream and detect: (1) whether the response contains a tool call (structured JSON) or a final answer (text). For tool calls, the framework waits for the complete JSON before executing the tool (partial tool-call JSON is invalid). For final answers, streaming can be forwarded directly to the user. OpenAI and Anthropic streaming APIs include delta events with finish_reason='tool_calls' to signal tool call completion. The loop then continues synchronously: execute the tool, append the result, start the next LLM call (which can also stream).

sources.label: Yao et al. (2022) — ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models, ICLR 2023
Wang et al. (2024) — A Survey on Large Language Model based Autonomous Agents
